architecture.summary: A proactive agent system that monitors user activity as a structured event stream (actor, verb, object, timestamp tuples) maintained by the operating system in graph form. A small temporal-graph-learning (TGL) model encodes this event stream, producing per-event trigger probabilities and per-entity routing scores in a single forward pass. Only when the trigger fires does the system invoke a downstream LLM to convert the structured handoff into a fluent user-facing sentence. The approach avoids the round-trip of rendering structured events as text for LLM decision-making and back again.

biblio.title: Do Proactive Agents Really Need an LLM to Decide When to Wake and What to Anchor?

claims.statement: TGL improves F1 on each of 14 backbones
TGL F1 improvement reaches up to +46.0 on some backbones
TGL runs at 11.13 ms per event on a GPU server
TGL runs at 13.99 ms per event on a consumer laptop
TGL is approximately 4–7x faster than every single-forward LLM-as-trigger configuration on GPU server
TGL is approximately 12–83x faster than every single-forward LLM-as-trigger configuration on consumer laptop
TGL checkpoint gives strongest trigger AUCs and most stable deployed threshold across trigger-architecture comparisons
TGL has approximately 220 MiB BF16 resident footprint deployable on-device
